name: Router Data Plane
description: Quiz focusing on hardware architecture, switching, TCAM and queues in routers.

questions.question: In network architecture, what is the main difference between a router's Data Plane and Control Plane?
options: The data plane establishes web sessions; the control plane checks bit errors on the wire.
The data plane acts locally in hardware; the control plane defines the end-to-end route.
The data plane runs globally in the cloud; the control plane acts physically on the cable.
The data plane encrypts the free payload; the control plane multiplexes the applications.

questions.question: Analyzing the structure of a router's 'Input Port', which vital functions occur sequentially in it?
options: Conversion of analog signals to fiber, data encryption and pure scheduling.
Generation of error-control packets, cloud routing and frame reassembly.
Execution of global routing protocols, malware blocking and port forwarding.
Physical line termination, link protocol and the lookup in the forwarding table.

questions.question: What are the three main types of 'Switching Elements' used in the manufacture of routers?
options: Switching via memory, via internal bus and via direct interconnection networks.
Switching via radio waves, via short coaxial cables and via local area networks.
Switching via global software, via clouds of servers and via long-haul networks.
Switching via multiplexing, via reserved circuits and via strictly web networks.

questions.question: In destination-based routing, how does the router use the 'Longest Prefix Matching' technique?
options: The router forwards the packet to the physical port that holds the very long structural cable.
The switch selects the table entry with the greatest number of matching initial bits.
The switch always prefers to send datagrams that have a much longer IP header.
The router cancels all video packets whose physical propagation time is long.

questions.question: What is the great practical advantage of using TCAM (Ternary Content Addressable Memory) in modern routers?
options: To temporarily store the whole payload of the datagram to perform bit-level fragmentation.
To encrypt the routes end to end on the network, protecting the core against cyberattacks.
To retrieve the address in a single clock cycle, regardless of the total table size.
To negotiate the three-way handshake with the core servers in order to reserve light bandwidth.

questions.question: What physically characterizes the severe problem known as Head-of-the-Line (HoL) blocking at the input ports?
options: The datagram held at the front of the input queue prevents the packets right behind it from advancing.
The packet stored in the output port's memory locks the flow-control requests there.
The physical transmission link abruptly cuts off sending due to excessive power loss.
The fragmentation process within the network blocks immediate reassembly at the output terminal.

questions.question: For exactly what reason did first-generation routers (based on traditional computers with Switching via Memory) have slow, limited rates?
options: The link suffers frequent thermal interruption, reducing the lifespan of the bare routing chip.
The CPU requires the prior handshake of the application layer, hugely increasing the link time.
The packet crosses the central bus twice, limiting the rate by the general memory bandwidth.
The switch encrypts each local datagram, immediately draining the routers' battery.

questions.question: How does the 'Switching via Interconnection Network' architecture (such as the multistage crossbar) scale its parallelism internally?
options: It requires building a secure tunnel to carry the data reliably right at the output.
It splits the datagram into fixed-size cells to switch them and reassemble them at the output.
It erases the parity bits of the IP header to violently speed up forwarding at the output.
It silently retransmits the frames in case of a collision physically detected at the output.

questions.question: Under exactly what condition will a router necessarily suffer packet losses (buffer overflow) at its output ports?
options: When the optical-fiber cable suffers serious damage that blocks the structural passage of frames.
When the hardware clock loses synchronization, requiring a reset of the main protocol.
When the fabric sends data at a rate that is higher than the transmission capacity of its link.
When the transport layer demands the immediate acknowledgement of a single multimedia flow.

questions.question: In managing the output-port queues, how do the FCFS (FIFO) and Round Robin scheduling policies work, respectively?
options: FCFS reorders packets based on payload size, and Round Robin drops data that clogs networks.
FCFS actively prioritizes audio during traffic time, and Round Robin delegates forwarding to Ethernet.
FCFS halts connections that lose data on the link, and Round Robin applies the base encryption.
FCFS dispatches in the strict temporal order of arrival, and Round Robin alternates turns between the queues.
